messages.content:
let's test your knowledge on "Seas and Ancient Mountains of North America" from "Deep Time: Discovering an Ancient Earth".

I will provide a question followed up by a number of points the question is worth. Majority of questions will be multiple-choice. 

Example:
Q."QUESTION"
1 points
- a 
- b
- c
etc.

Do you understand the task at hand ?
As an AI language model, I don't have any understanding. However, I can perform the task as per your instructions.

Q. Even by the early 1800's, scientists had very little direct evidence or
knowledge concerning the:
1 point
-approximate distances to planets and stars.
-laws governing motion of planets and stars.
-actual chemical composition of stars.
The correct answer is: actual chemical composition of stars.

Q. Similar to the once seemingly unattainable knowledge about stars,
geologists can reach back, and rather amazingly, unravel quite a bit
about earth's deep history. The key to deciphering this history is the
analysis of:
1 point
-ancient books.
-isotopes.
-rocks and rock layering
The correct answer is: rocks and rock layering.

Q. Rocks deposited from air or water orice are called:
1 point
-metamorphic
-sedimentary
-igneous
The correct answer is: sedimentary.

Q. Rocks that have undergone recrystallization, as a consequence of
exposure to heat and/or pressure, are called:
1 point
-igneous
-sedimentary
-metamorphic
The correct answer is: metamorphic.

Q. Rocks that were once molten and then solidified, via cooling and
crystallization, are called:
1 point
-igneous
-sedimentary.
-metamorphic.
The correct answer is: igneous.

Q. Which type of rock is really the key to understanding past surface
environments (e.g., distinguishing between things like wind-blown
dunes or rivers or beaches or glaciers)?
1 point
-sedimentary
-metamorphic
-igneous
The correct answer is: sedimentary.

Q. Solidification of igneous rock BELOW surface results in slow cooling. These so-called "intrusive igneous" rocks are characterized by [?] mineral/grain size.
1 point
-exceptionally small (microscopic or sub-microscopic)
-relatively large (naked-eye-visible)
-a wide variety of
The correct answer is: relatively large (naked-eye-visible).

Q. On a geologic time chart, the boundaries between demarcations like
eras and periods are not rounded off to easy to use or easy to remember
values. For example, the boundary between the Jurassic and
Cretaceous is at 145.5 MY. It seems like it would make sense to just call it
150 MY.
The REASON that the demarcations are not necessarily whole numbers,
or rounded off, is that:
1 point
-they represent the timing of rise and fall of past oceans
-they are based on appearance and disappearance of life.
-they are matched up with global warming and cooling
cycles.
The correct answer is: they are based on appearance and disappearance of life.

Q. Snow-ball earth is a colloquial term for a major cooling event in earth history. It took place [?], and involved [?]
1 point
-in the earliest Precambrian (Archean), a dramatic cooling of
the mantle.
-near the end of Precambrian time, equatorial glaciers.
-during the Pleistocene, the advent of creatures like wooly
mammoths.
The correct answer is: near the end of Precambrian time, equatorial glaciers.

Q. The Burgess Shale is a time-capsule of life from the early Cambrian
Period, at the beginning of the Paleozoic Era.
Fossils from this locality are
and represent
1 point
-poorly preserved, some of the first amphibians on planet
earth.
-very well preserved, the first macroscopic organisms
(bigger than a single cell).
-very well preserved, the first vestiges of modern phyla
(body-plans).
The correct answer is: very well preserved, the first macroscopic organisms (bigger than a single cell).

Q. The Burgess Shale is a time-capsule of life from the early Cambrian Period, at the beginning of the Paleozoic Era.
Fossils from this locality are [?] and represent [?]
1 point
-poorly preserved, some of the first amphibians on planet earth.
-very well preserved, the first macroscopic organisms
(bigger than a single cell).
-very well preserved, the first vestiges of modern phyla (body-plans).
This seems to be a repeat of the previous question. The answer is "very well preserved, the first macroscopic organisms (bigger than a single cell)."

Q. A key feature of North America (and beyond) is a rock-layer-boundary
termed the Great Unconformity. This feature is:
1 point
-a time-gap with rocks above being vastly younger than
rocks below.
-a zone of heating, where igneous rocks below cooked
overlying layers.
-a source of exceptional fossils from the late Precambrian.
The correct answer is: a time-gap with rocks above being vastly younger than rocks below.

Q. The Great Unconformity, as with nearly all geological unconformities, is
probably linked to both:
1 point
-flooding the continent with ocean water.
-erosion and uplift.
-sedimentation and sinking
The correct answer is: erosion and uplift.

Q. Paleo-geographic reconstructions involve analyzing rock magnetism.
Rocks retain vestiges of the magnetic field at the time of their
formation.
Rocks that formed at high geographic latitude (i.e., polar) will exhibit:
1 point
-shallow (near horizontal) magnetic dip component
-unusually weak magnetism.
-steep (near vertical) magnetic dip component.
The correct answer is: steep (near vertical) magnetic dip component.

Q. A hallmark of the Phanerozoic (i.e., the Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and
Cenozoic) in North America has been:
1 point
-a distinctive lack of any major mountain building episodes.
-an oscillation of climate producing numerous pulses of
glacial ice into the continent.
-various transgressions of ocean water onto the continent.
The correct answer is: various transgressions of ocean water onto the continent.

Q. Mountain building on a global scale occurs at the end of the Paleozoic.
Much of this is linked to:
1 point
-widespread climate change.
-rifting of the Pangea super-continent.
-continent-collision with the accumulation of the Pangea
super-continent.
The correct answer is: continent-collision with the accumulation of the Pangea super-continent.

Q. Features of the Late Paleozoic Fountain Formation (i.e., the rock which
comprises the Flatirons) that suggest erosion of a nearby mountain
range (i.e., the Ancestral Rocky Mountains) include:
1 point
-conglomerates and feldspar rich sediment.
-large amounts of lava-derived sediment.
-metamorphism due to pressure of overlying mountains
The correct answer is: conglomerates and feldspar rich sediment.

Q. Deposition of the Fountain Formation occurred within what sort of
sedimentary environment?
1 point
-desert settings
-high mountain glacial settings
-river and stream settings
The correct answer is: river and stream settings.

Q. One key piece of information derived from the Fountain Formation and
correlative (i.e., similar age) units like the Maroon and Cutler
Formations is the:
1 point
-location of the Ancestral Rocky Mountain uplifts.
-precise paleo-temperatures of both atmosphere and epeiric
seas at that time.
-depth of epeiric seas in North America
Previous
Submit
The correct answer is: location of the Ancestral Rocky Mountain uplifts.
Congratulations you have completed the task. You have scored 94.44%
Thank you! It was my pleasure to assist you.
